elements.sent-2411-001: We analyze the effect that online algorithms have on the environment that they are learning.
elements.sent-2411-002: As a motivation, consider recommendation systems that use online algorithms to learn optimal product recommendations based on user and product attributes.
elements.sent-2411-003: It is well known that the sequence of recommendations affects user preferences.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, typical learning algorithms treat the user attributes as static and disregard the impact of their recommendations on user preferences.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our interest is to analyze the effect of this mismatch between the model assumption of a static environment, and the reality of an evolving environment affected by the recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-006: To perform this analysis, we first introduce a model for a generic coupled evolution of the parameters that are being learned, and the environment that is affected by it.
elements.sent-2411-007: We then frame a linear bandit recommendation system (RS) into this generic model where the users are characterized by a state variable that evolves based on the sequence of recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-008: The learning algorithm of the RS does not explicitly account for this evolution and assumes that the users are static.
elements.sent-2411-009: A dynamical system model that captures the coupled evolution of the population state and the learning algorithm is described, and its equilibrium behavior is analyzed.
elements.sent-2411-010: We show that when the recommendation algorithm is able to learn the population preferences in the presence of this mismatch, the algorithm induces similarity in the preferences of the user population.
elements.sent-2411-011: In particular, we present results on how different properties of the recommendation algorithm, namely the user attribute space and the exploration-exploitation tradeoff, effect the population preferences when they are learned by the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-012: We demonstrate these results using model simulations.
elements.sent-2411-013: Online learning algorithms learn an environment through a sequence of actions and feedbacks from the environment to these actions.
elements.sent-2411-014: Most online learning algorithms make the key assumption that the characteristics that describe the environment, and the statistics of its response to the actions do not change as the learning progresses, i.e., the environment is static.
elements.sent-2411-015: Specifically, there is an implicit assumption that the environment does not respond to the actions of the learner.
elements.sent-2411-016: We argue that that is a strong assumption for these algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-017: Our interest in this paper is to analyze the consequence of this mismatch.
elements.sent-2411-018: Specifically, we consider the case of a learning algorithm updating its parameters assuming a stationary environment while the environment is evolving in response to the actions of the learning process, i.e., unbeknownst to it, the learner is influencing the environment that it is learning.
elements.sent-2411-019: Thus, we have an unintended consequence on the environment.
elements.sent-2411-020: This is perhaps best illustrated by a `YouTube session' where a user starts off seeking videos on specific topics but the recommendations distract from the original intent and take the user through an entirely different sequence of videos.
elements.sent-2411-021: And all this while, the recommendation system would be updating the interest profile of the user in response to the new clicks.
elements.sent-2411-022: Thus the learning algorithm (recommendation system) and the environment (user preferences) are evolving together.
elements.sent-2411-023: In this paper, we will analyze this coupled evolution of the state of the learner and the state of the environment.
elements.sent-2411-024: Learning in non-stationary environments has received attention in recent literature, especially in multi-armed bandits where reward distributions of the arms are assumed non-stationary.
elements.sent-2411-025: In these models, the objective is typically to develop a learning algorithm that performs optimally in some sense, in the presence of an environment that changes.
elements.sent-2411-026: For example, in \cite{besbes2014stochastic , the expected reward from any arm can change at any point and any number of times but the changes are independent of the algorithm and the reward sequence.}
elements.sent-2411-027: In rotting and recharging bandits, respectively \cite{levine2017rotting and \cite{kleinberg2018recharging , the arms have memory of when they were last recommended and the reward distribution depends on the delay since the last use of the arm.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: A generalization of rotting and recharging bandits is considered in \cite{fiez2018multi where the mean rewards from the arms vary according to a Markov chain.}
elements.sent-2411-029: The change of user preferences in response to recommendations has been well documented \cite{Flaxman2016filter , \cite{mansoury2020feedback .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: This has inspired some work to explicitly model this interaction and exploit it to improve the learning, e.g., \cite{shah2018bandit and more recently \cite{Agarwal2024onlinerecommendationsagentsdiscounted .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: These works focus on optimally learning user preferences as they change, which is clearly different from our goal.
elements.sent-2411-032: There is some literature that studies the effect of algorithms on the environment, albeit in a non online learning setting.
elements.sent-2411-033: The performative prediction literature \cite{perdomo2020performative studies learning the environment when the objective of the learner affects the environment, i.e., the environment `performs' as a reaction to what learner does; see \cite{hardt2022performative , \cite{hardt2023performative for more recent work on performative prediction.}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: In this work, learning the environment is studied in a supervised learning setting.
elements.sent-2411-035: The work on data feedback loops \cite{taori2023data induced by data generated by learning algorithms is also similar in nature.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Feedback loops in collaborative filtering based recommendation systems is studied \cite{sinha2016deconvolving and \cite{schmit2018human also discuss the problem from a recommendation systems perspective.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: While these works are similar in nature, the model that we use in our work is significantly different.
elements.sent-2411-038: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first analysis of mismatch between the learner and the environment.
elements.sent-2411-039: The rest of the paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-040: We first introduce a generic model of interaction between an online learning algorithm and its environment in which the environment changes in response to the learners actions.
elements.sent-2411-041: We provide a result that allows us to understand the asymptotic behavior of the model by analyzing a dynamical system, when suitable assumptions are made on the model.
elements.sent-2411-042: Then, we consider the particular case of the contextual linear bandits problem in the setting where the environment is influenced by the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-043: This is followed by a discussion on the equilibrium points of the dynamical system of aforementioned linear bandits setting and various results obtained from the simulation of the same.
elements.sent-2411-044: The proofs for all the theorems are provided in the appendix.
elements.sent-2411-045: In the rest of the paper the term `learner' will refer to the online learning algorithm that assumes that the environment is stationary.
elements.sent-2411-046: Also, we will use the terms `learner', `algorithm' and `learning algorithm' interchangeably.
elements.section-2411-002: A Model of a Generic Online Learning System
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{general-model-schematic.png} \caption{Schematic of the general model of interaction between the learner $L$ and the environment $E.$ The numbers in the picture correspond to the step numbers in the generic model description in Section~\ref{sec:2-general-model-description}.} \label{fig:general-model-illustration}
elements.sent-2411-047: We consider a discrete time interaction sequence between an environment $E$ and a learner $L$ that is trying to learn $E;$ time is indexed by $t.$ The learner believes that the environment is characterized by a state $\varphi^\ast \in \lstates,$ where $\lstates$ is the set of all possible states that the learner knows.
elements.sent-2411-048: The learning of $E$ by $L$ is achieved by finding $\varphi^\ast$ that minimizes objective function $\mathcal{L^O ,$ i.e., \begin{equation}\varphi^\ast = \min_\varphi \mathcal{L^O}(\varphi).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-049: However, the environment is characterized by $\psi_t \in \estates$ at time $t,$ where $\estates$ is the set of all possible states of the environment.
elements.sent-2411-050: To learn $E,$ $L$ responds to a signal from $E$ at time $t$ with an action.
elements.sent-2411-051: This action elicits a reward from $E$ even as $E$ changes state to $\psi_t \in \estates$ that depends on the action by $L.$ The sequence of past signals, actions, and rewards are used to determine the action at time $t;$ this history is captured by $L$ in its state variable $\varphi_t \in \lstates.$ In the following, we make the sequence of events of this interaction between $L$ and $E$ explicit and also introduce the notation.
elements.sent-2411-052: At time $t,$ the learner receives a random signal $U_t$ from the environment.
elements.sent-2411-053: The distribution of $U_t$ is a function of the state of the environment $\psi_t.$.
elements.sent-2411-054: The learner responds with an action $A_t = Q (U_t, \varphi_t)$, where $Q$ is a random variable that describes the output of the learning algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-055: The environment responds to the action of the learner by returning a reward $R_t = r (U_t, A_t, \psi_t) + \eta_t$, where $r$ is the reward function and $\eta_t$ is noise with $\mathbb{E [\eta_t |U_t, A_t, \psi_t] = 0.$ The tuple $(U_t,A_t)$ can be interpreted as the context that influences the reward at time $t.$.}
elements.sent-2411-056: The learner updates its state from $\varphi_{t}$ as \begin{equation}\varphi_{t + 1} = \varphi_t + \alpha_t \Phi (U_t, A_t, R_t, \varphi_t). \label{eq:algo-evolution}\end{equation} where $\Phi$ describes the updating function that the learner uses and $\alpha_t$ is a scalar constant, also called the learning parameter.
elements.sent-2411-057: Typically, this update corresponds to the minimization of a loss function or a surrogate objective function $\loss$, which is different from $\mathcal{L^O ,$ and can be written as \begin{equation}\label{eq:learner-objective} \varphi_{t+1} = \arg\min_{\varphi} \loss(\varphi, A_{1:t}, R_{1:t})\end{equation} where $A_{1:t}$ and $R_{1:t}$ are sequences of actions and rewards until time $t.$ Such a practice of iteratively optimizing a surrogate function is common in online learning algorithms, when optimizing over the desired objective function is not feasible \cite{lange2000optimization .}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Finally, the environment reacts to the action $A_t$ of the learner by changing its state to $\psi_{t + 1}$ as follows. \begin{equation}\psi_{t + 1} = \psi_t + \beta_t \Psi (U_t, A_t, R_t, \psi_t). \label{eq:env-evolution}\end{equation} Here $\Psi$ is the updating function of the environment and $\beta_t$ is a scalar constant that we call the adaptation parameter.
elements.sent-2411-059: In Figure~\ref{fig:general-model-illustration we give a compact representation of the above steps at time $t$.}
elements.sent-2411-060: There are two important features of the preceding description that are significantly different from the usual assumptions in the literature on multi-armed bandits and other similar online algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-061: In step 5 above, the environment reacts to the action of the learner and changes its state.
elements.sent-2411-062: Thus the environment is not static and the learning algorithm will influence the state evolution of the environment through the choice of its action sequence.
elements.sent-2411-063: This model is similar in nature to that of \cite{jiang2019degenerate , and there are similarities to reinforcement learning models as well.}
elements.sent-2411-064: The key differences are twofold: we allow $\lstates$ and $\estates,$ the state space of $L$ and $E$ respectively, to be different, i.e., the learner does not know the actual description of $E,$ and we use the notion of model mismatch between the learner and the environment.
elements.sent-2411-065: We formally define mismatch using the following.
elements.sent-2411-066: The environment is stationary if the adaptability parameter $\beta_t$ satisfies \begin{equation}\beta_t = 0 \quad \forall t=0,1,2,\ldots \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-068: The environment is learnable if there exists a surjection from $\lstates$ to $\estates$ i.e., if there exists a function $f$ such that for any environment state $\psi \in \estates$, there exists at least one learner state $\varphi \in \lstates$ that satisfies \begin{equation}\psi = f(\varphi).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-070: However, when $E$ does not satisfy either of the above conditions, $L$ is not guaranteed to learn $E.$ We show this using the following two examples.
elements.sent-2411-071: Consider an environment where $\estates = \lstates$ but $\beta_t \neq 0$ for all $t.$ An adversary, at some time $t',$ can change the state of the environment immediately after $L$ learns $E$ with high confidence.
elements.sent-2411-072: Consider another example where $\beta_t = 0$ for all $t$ but $\estates = \R^d$ and $\lstates = \R^e$ for some $d>e.$ In such a case, minimizing any objective function will not help $L$ learn $E,$ as the state of $E$ lies in a higher dimensional space as compared to the state of $L.$ We use both of the above ideas in formally defining mismatch below.
elements.sent-2411-073: A mismatch between the learning algorithm and the environment is said to exist if the environment is either non-stationary or non-learnable or both.
elements.sent-2411-074: Our objective in this paper is to analyze the unintended consequences on the environment when the learner uses a mismatched model for the environment, i.e., when $\lstates \neq \estates,$ and also assumes that it is static, i.e., $\beta_t=0$ for all $t.$ It is also of interest to understand if the learner is be able to learn the environment despite the mismatch, and what happens to the environment in that case.
elements.sent-2411-075: The following definition formalizes this idea.
elements.sent-2411-076: The learner is said to asymptotically track the environment if \begin{equation}\lim_{t \rightarrow \infty} \varphi_t = \min_\varphi \mathcal{L^O(\varphi)} = \varphi_\ast\end{equation} for some $\varphi_\ast \in \lstates.$.
elements.sent-2411-077: If the learner asymptotically tracks the environment when there is a mismatch, it appears as if the mismatch never existed from the learner's perspective.
elements.sent-2411-078: Moreover, the environment is affected by its interaction with the learner.
elements.sent-2411-079: By analyzing the long-term behavior of the model, we can understand what happens to the environment when the learner asymptotically tracks the environment.
elements.sent-2411-080: We characterize the long-term consequences of the mismatch by analyzing the asymptotic behavior of the states of the learner and of the environment.
elements.sent-2411-081: We make the following assumptions on the model.
elements.sent-2411-082: While these assumptions are necessary for mathematical analysis, the qualitative results surrounding the analysis still hold under small relaxations of these assumptions i.e., models with $\alpha_t \approx \beta_t$ and algorithms with continuous approximations of discrete probability distributions.
elements.sent-2411-083: These assumptions let us use stochastic approximation theory to obtain the desired system of Ordinary Differential Equations (o.d.e.), which gives us the following result.
elements.sent-2411-084: We do so by obtaining a system of o.d.e.s that asymptotically tracks the model, using stochastic approximation theory \cite{borkar2009stochastic , and analyze the asymptotic behavior of the o.d.e.. system.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Under the assumptions \ref{assumption:1 , A2, and A3, the sequences $\{ \varphi_t \}$ and $\{ \psi_t \}$ converge to a connected internally chain transitive invariant set of the o.d.e.s \begin{equation}\dot{\varphi} (\tau) =\mathbb{E} [\Phi | \varphi, \psi] \text{ and } \dot{\psi} (\tau) =\mathbb{E} [\Psi | \varphi, \psi] \label{eq:general-model-ode}\end{equation} respectively a.s.}
elements.sent-2411-086: This result tells us that we can characterize the long term behavior of the states of $L$ and $E,$ given by $\varphi_t$ and $\psi_t$ respectively, by analyzing the long term behavior of continuous time iterates of the o.d.e.
elements.sent-2411-087: This helps us study the long term behavior of the complex stochastic model using dynamical system theory, which has been well studied.
elements.sent-2411-088: We will now use this model of interaction between a learner and its environment to study the special case of the interaction between a recommendation platform and its users.
